CURRENT AFFAIRS.

It will be remembered that in our Christmas Number
we, drew attention to No. 58 Claremont Terrace,
Harold’s Cross, ab. which address, according to Thom’s
Directory, the following have quarters:—Viggo
Schwartz, expert; the Continental Agency Co., and J.
Hanson Wendlehoe. Viggo Schwartz, we pointed out,
was egg and poultry market expert to the I.A.0.8., and
from the house of which he is one of the occupiers a
good deal of circular touting, and we have no doubt
some business, in continental eggs and margarine, was
done. We wrote:—Perhaps the organ of the
I.A.0.8., Ltd., the Irish Homestead, would throw a
little light ‘on the matter.” :

‘We have carefully glanced through our contemporary
of December 16th for some reference or explanation of
this matter, but fail to find ‘any. Has the Homestead
nothing to say for itself and its expert? If the expert
has any explanation to offer, and if the Homestead,

cither through  hatred of thought or incapacity to
think,’” does not feel competent o deal with the matter
we will give reasonable space to Viggo Schwartz, expert,
for any explanation he may have to offer. Viggo
Schwartz was an expert in the employment of a public
body—the T.A.0.S.—now some way or other connected
with the Deopartment (Scotch), and the public are en-
titled to some explanation concerning the state of
affairs that we briefly disclosed in our issue of Decem-
ber 9th.

On Monday, when the foregoing notes had been set
up, we received the following letter from  the Irish
Federated Poultry Sccicties, Lid.:—

The Irish Federated Ponltry Societies, Ltd., Shippers
selected Irish new laid eggs, and fresh killed

Telegraphic address: “XKarka, Dublin.”

22 Lincoln Place, Dublin,
18th December, 1905.

Sir, I am desired by the Directors of the Irish Feder-
ated Poultry Societies, Ltd., to thank you for having
brought under their notice the circumstances referred to
in your issue of the 9th inst., with regard to Mr. Viggo
Schwartz, who is now employed by this Society, and to
say that, having fully investigated the case, they are
satisfied that no business connection exists between Mr.
Schwartz and Mr. J. Hansen-Wendelboe, Peterson Bros.
or their principals. Having regard to the nature of the
business in which Messrs. Wendelboe and Peterson are
engaged, it is, in the opinion of the Directors, unde-
sirable that those gentlemen should continue to share
the same house, even as a residence, with their country-
man, Mr. Schwartz. The latter has acquiesced in the
wishes of the Directors, to which he will give immediate
effect,—Yours faithfully, Deryop O’BFIEN,

President.

It appears from this letter that Mr. Viggo Schwartz
is now an employee of the Irish Federated Poultry So-
cicties, Ltd., 22 Lincoln place, and we understand from
informal information that he left the service of the
T.A.0.8., 22 Lincoln Place, several months ago. We
have looked up Thom’s Directory for 1905, and at the
address 22 Lincoln Place, there is no mention of the
Irish Federated Poultry Societies, Ltd., so that it must
be a rather newly born Corporation. The Directors
of the latter state that “ having fully investigated the
case, they are satisfied that no business connection exists
botween Mr. Schwartz and Mr. J. Hansen-Wendelboe,
Peterson Bros., or their principals.” Well, be that as
it may, we are in a position to state clearly that as far
back “as about the middle of last year Mr. Viggo
Schwartz of 58 Claremont Terrace, Harold’s Cross, Dub-
lin, contemplated taking up an agency for a Continental
firm. Did he take up that agency? If he did not, at
least he was on the look out for it. If he wanted in
1904 to run a Continental agency along with carry-
ing on his business as “ expert ** to the I4A.O.§., it is
certainly a curious coincidence to find in the Directory
of 1905, that a concern by the name of “The Con-
tinental Agency Co.”’ shared the occupancy of 58 Clare-
mont Terrace, Harold’s Cross, Dublin, along with him.

It is something that Mr. Viggo Schwartz, imported
“ oxpert,”’ has been requested to send Messrs. Wendel-
boe and Peterson from his residence; but we think that
moro explanation is necessary. Wae say explicitly that
in 1904, he, whilst in the employment as “ expert *’ of
the I.A.O.S., was endeavouring to work a Continental
Agency. As he apparently admits that it is not de-
sirable that he should share the same Tesidence with

countrymen of his who are Continental agents, he must.

BIE LEADER™

admit that it was most undesirable that he, whilst in
the employment of the I.A.0.S., should seek to procure
2 Continental agency himself.

Now it is bad enough to be importing “ experts ”” over
‘here in shoals, without * experts,’”” wlhen they get well

A delegate to the recent United Irish League Con-
vention writes to us:—“ Being a constant reader of
your splendid little paper, I thought I would report to
you the following—Last week when I was returning
from the Mansion House Convention on my way to
Kingsbridge Station, I purchased the Lraper, and as
T looked at the cover, I saw Peterson’s advertisement for
pipes; it just struck me that T should bring one to my
iriend at home; the.advertisement stated they were to
be had at all high-class shops; I immediately made for
-one—asked for a pipe. The attendant asked me what
brand I wanted; I said Peterson’s. He informed me
he hadn’t any of that make, and wanted me to take
some other. I refused, and came to the next shop, re-
peated my question; the attendant here asked what
price pipe I wanted ; I told him I did not mind as long
as I got what I wanted. He said he had different
brands, but did not stock Peterson’s. I left by making
the remark— You don’t, because they are Irish.’
“Well, sir, T came to the third high-class house, and was
informed they would send out for them ; but it was now
‘train time, and I had to leave Dublin after trying three
big shops for an article that I believe is made there,
and could not get it.—Yours, Disappointed.  East
Cork.”

Seldom, if ever, were so many commercial men
gathered together in Glasnevin cemetery to pay their
last respects to the deceased wife of a colleague than
assembled on Friday morning last, when the wife of
Mr. D. F. McCarthy was buried. Mr. McCarthy is one
of the most popular men “on the road,” and as he is
closely associated with an Irish industry which bas made
rapid progress in recent years, it is not out of place
on our part to offer him our sincere sympathy in a very
sad bereavement. Mr. Dan McCarthy, as traveller for
Morroughs of Douglas, Cork, the well known cloth
manufacturers, is closely connected with the pust
of one of our most important and buoyant indu
and so, in a manner, belongs very much to the Irish
Industrial Revival. The News of the death of M
McCarthy came as a sudden shock to her many friend
and to the vast number of the friends of her husband
all over the country. Both were familiar figures at
functions comnected with the Language Revival, and
none more keenly grieve the untimely death of Mrs.
McCarthy than several prominently identified with the
Trvish Ireland movement.

Which of the highly-paid auditors of the Local Go-
vernment Board looks after the accounts of the Car-

shortly have an opportunity of distinguishing himself
after the manner of his colleague, Mr. Baker, who
checks the long tots of Wicklow. At a recent meeting
of the Carrick-on-Suir Urban Council it was unani-
mously decided on the motion of Dr. Murphy, seconded
by Mr. Conway, to put up the names of the streets of
the town in the Irish language. We wonder did the
Local Government Board ever hear the old saying—
'.‘ Carrick, T dread you?’ The Councillors also decided,
if the auditor should surcharge the members, to fight it
oub with him and his Board. The auditor had better
pub his armour on, and take heed of the war-cry—
“ Carrick, T dread youl’’:

Mr. J. M. Wall refers to the one and only occasion on
which he and the editor of this paper met each other.
We remember the occasion well. Mr. Wall had been
making a tour in Ireland some years ago, and he
dropped into the offices of the LEapER. We confess we
took him rather brusquely, and fearing that he might
be—like some Irish-Americans—in too great a conceit
of his adopted land, we opened out freely and in plain
language on America and Americans. There is one
thing which we have in.common with many Americans—
we have experience of life and knowledge of the world,
and as Mr. J. M.Wall is an experienced as well as a
thinking man, we are not surprised to find that our in-
terview with him in no way prejudiced him against ms.
Quite the reverse. Now had he been one of our Sweet
Boy Graduates, and had we talked to him as we did, he
would probably .have been calling us a “coward,” a
““ traitor,”” and other dreadful names ever since. But
Mr. J. M. Wall is a distinguished American journalist,
2 thinking man, an experienced man, and a sensible
man. Having paid us a very high compliment, he re-
fers to the solitary interview that he had with us in

And this with no idea of fevoritism in the least, for when I met Mor

Having made some more quotations from the book,

In the course of an article published in the New Ire-
land Review, long before the LEADER started, the Editor
of this paper incidently remarked—“Let us have as
many papers as people choose to start and others are
willing to buy.” We say the same to-day. If some
people start papers that other people won’t buy, so
much the worse for some people. They can shut up
shop, or try the experiment of swopping Editors. We
refer to this as we observe that a young gentleman who
had control of a journalistic venture that apparently
has been a drug on the market, took occasion on his
leaving the editorial chair, in which, notwithstanding
his varied accomplishments, he was not a wild success,
to treat us to an unconsciously comic eruption of what
we may call ¢ cultured ”’ Biddy-Moriartyism. We en-
joyed the volcano, and we are grateful for the compli-
ment ; it is not given to everyone to be advertised even
to a limited circle by a volcanic eruption.

The young gentleman represented us as having said
in the New Ireland Review article to which we have
already referred that “ we nceded in Ireland many new:
papers written from many points of view.”’ Hitching
on this misquotation to the fact that we often referred
to the boycott of ourselves by the papers, the irate young
gentleman concludes, by what process of logic we, crawl-
ing croatuves that we ave, are not * intellectual **
enough to perceive, that we were under “a double
pledge ”’ to take cognisance of his journalistic dismal
failure! The judgment as well as the temper of this
young gontleman, we fear, must have boiled over on
the hob of our indifference. Now it was ridiculous to.
ignore the IEapEr, for even its enemies are compelled
to admit that the Leaper made history ; might we in a.
whisper suggest that there are some papers that it is a.
positive act of charity to ignore? Let as many as will
start papers by all means, and let them keep {hem
going if people will buy them; if people will not huy
them—uwell, there’s an end to them. Where’s the use
of lething the public know that the disappointed occu-

- R

pit.sr of the editorial chair is in a white rage over his
failure? We admit that it is natural to feel annoyed
under the distressing circumstances, but if an infurjated
young man will insist on letting fly in an uncontrolled
rage, why not ease himself by breaking the crockery
ware, and flinging the pots and pans about in the pri-
vacy of his own apartments? Is it wise to let outsiders
look on? Inexperienced young men should cultivate the
habit of counting ten before writing unconsciously
comic and infuriated articles. In the first article in
our first number we remarked that “ we fortunately look
for the support of the public on our  merits, and the
merits of our case, and not on the puffs of our con-
temporaries.”” . As we have merits the lack of pufls only
amused us, and made subjects for entertaining “ copy.’
We faced the situation on the ‘“as-good-and-as-cheap
general principle, and harboured no foolish hopes unless
we were able to give solid value to the public for their
pennies. Sentimentalists who expect the members of
the public to pay a penny for a weak farthing’s worth
have much to learn from hard experience.

We have tackled some rough mastiffs, and other
wicked animals in our time—we tackle them to-day—
and it naturally amuses us to observe a little poodle dog.
preparatory to running off with itself, snarling and
barking, and wagging its tail excitedly, and trying to
convince itself that we are afraid of it!

In the course of years it is to be expected that some of
our “intellectual ’ young men may become so highly
“ cultured *’ that they may evolve into a serene state in
which they can live and thrive on an exclusive regimen
of weak poetry and water. We note that in a dialogue
in this puissant “ Long-Felt Want,”’ one of the talkers
remarks that ZLeaderism is “ the apotheosis of the animal
man.”’” How dreadful!  Leaderites should all go at
once and hang themselves. We suppose it is not “tone’’
to be an animal in highly respectable “ cultured’’
circles—one should rather be a sort of a spook in
whiskers and knickerbockers. But might we be per-
mitted to say—if an “animal man,”’ whatever that is,
dare presume to say anything—might we be permitted
to say that as we happen to be of the male sex, we would
prefer to be the ““apotheosis of the animal man’’ than
the apotheosis of the male “ old woman?”’

A reader of ours—“ A Student '’—sends us a sugges-
tion for a new game. He writes:— Irish Forfeits—A
New Game.—Here is a new game which may prove
amusing and instructive for the winter evenings. All
the players must be students of Irish. They should all
sit round in a semi-circle as at the usual game of for-
feits. The first person says an Irish verb, the second
another parf of speech, the third another; but each
person’s word must make sense, and fit in grammatically
with those that have preceded it. The person who fails
(on one or more occasions according to arrangement) to
find a suitable word must pay a forfeit. The players
should try to keep the sentences open as long as possible
by introducing dependent sentences. When, however,
the sense is complete the person next in rotation should
open with a new verb. In the end the forfeits should
be given one after the other to the persons who
can tell their names in Irish. The order of answering
might be determined by placing all the names (of the
players) in a hat, and drawing them out at random.
It may be objected that this game is more instructive
than amusing. Well, be it so, there are many Lea-
guers, I’'m sure, who would just as soon be instructed
as amused. It would, at least, if adopted, have the
effect of introducing spoken Irish more than it is to our
homes.””

Just now many people are no doubt developing a
lively interest in railway time-bables in view of the

THE COMING EVANT.—IL

g HE coming event has in part arrived, and in

] such fashion, too, as promises the arrival of the
other half. By showing themselves able to form not
merely a Cabinet, but a brilliant one (as Cabinets now
20), the Liberals have shattered many of the hopes of
their opponents. Had C.-B. proved unable to form a
Ministry, Mr. Balfour would have been able to say to
the clectorate—“ Will you give power to a Party like
that?”’  But the “hopeless disorganisation of the
Liberals,”” on which the Tories had built their main
hopes, turns out to have been largely a myth. The
truth is, each Party has its disturber; in one, Rose-
bery, and in the other, Joe. But Rosebery is a feeble
mischief-maker in comparison with Joe, and the noble
lord is now isolated, since an able Cabinet has been
formed without him. Now all these things may seem
purely British matters, but they concern us so vitally
that we must not ignore them. The new Cabinet is
far ‘more capable than that which has gone out, and
by forming it- C.-B. has proved two things—(1) That
the “ disorganisation of the Liberals’’ is mainly a Tory
fiction, and (2) that he can give the British electorate
much better value in the way of a Ministry than the
Tories can. All this is bound to have a great influence
upon the coming election. The Liberals, proved cap-
able of forming a competent Cabinet, have twice as
good a chance as they would have had, had t.heu*
Cabinst-making power not been tested, and five times
a better chance than if, having tried, they had failed
to make a Ministry. Now, it is dangerous to prophesy
without knowing, but one success is earnest of another,
and it scems safe to forecast that the Liberals will come
into power (fov they ave only <n office at pl'esc:m;): as
the result of the General Blection. But I feel np:hued
to go farther than that, for I suspect that the Liberals
will come into offico with what Feap eite truly says
they have been “ praying to God*’ for—an independent
majority. In that case we may, as I have previously
said, beg for crumbs. As for Home Rule—we may
whistle for it.  The soft, green Irishman may not
understand  this, but let him remember that every
Tiberal is also an Englishman, and an Englishman
first. Any English party not dependent on Ivish aid
will treat us—IEnglishly. 3

But, saith the green Irishman, Home Rulc. is before
the country! Isit? That is hardly my reading of the
situation. If the Cabinet unitedly declared in favour

of Home Rule on every day between this and the com-
ing olections, the House of Lords would say when the
Bill reached them that Home Rule had not been before
the country. If the Cabinet said, we will introduce the
Bill of '86, or the Bill of ’93 as prosented to the House
of Lords, then the Ministry could indisputably claim
that Home Rule had been before the country. A Bill
is a Bill, but “Home Rule’—to the British oar-—is
simply a phrase. To have Home Rule “ bofore the
country,”” the least that tho Liberals could do would
be' to lay down the leading lincs of the matter. The
crux of cruces is generally admifted to be the rotention
-question—that is, whether there shall still be an Irish
wepresentation at Westminster, and if so, to what ex-
tent! In fact, the general scope and aim of the con-
templated measure must needs be defined and dc-
Timited; the powers of the proposed Assembly must
be shown, and its relation to English and Imperial in-
‘terests shadowed forth—else the Iouse of Lords
say that Home Rule was not before the clectors. Into
the morality or sincerity of this attitude there is not
the least use in entering. What happened before would
happen again. The sot, the imbecile, the .dotard, the
paralytic, the temporarily-released victim of dementia,
would be once more brought to swell the ranks of the
more sane and reputable bigots banded together to vote
down a nation’s claims.

Well, somebody will say, what is there to prevent the
Liberals from putting the main points of their Home
Rule plan before the country?  Several things. In
the first place, I don’t believe they have any such plan
—or intend having any. In the next place, if they get
that “ independent majority,”” which is only too likely.
they can let Irish legislation slide. But there is an-
other element likely to act as a shunter of Home Rule—
‘the Liberal Unionists. If the Liberal Party once got
‘their independent majority, and declared “off’’ with
Home Rule, the Liberal Unionists would soon return to
the fold, and then the old order would be restored with
a vengeance. It is doubtful if anything more than a
minority, pessibly a small minority, of the Likerals nov
cling to Gladstonian Home Rule.  Therefore, if My
Morley and his friends seceded, owing to the abandon-
ment of Home Rule, their loss might be more than
counterbalanced—not, of course, morally, but numeri-
cally—by the return of the Liberal Unionists. These
considerations will help to show how many dangers stand
in the way of the Irish Party “ holding the balance ’’ in
the next Parliament. My fear is that they will hold
no such balance. Even if they should hold it, the re-
sulting advantages will be far smaller than our unre-
flecting people anticipate. The situation, indeed, may
be summed up thus—The Liberals will not put Home
Rule definitely before the country, hoping they can get
Zheir “independent majority.”” If they get it, we are
dished. If they do not get it, then we have command—
but command over a Party that has not put Home Rule
definitely before the electors. In the former case we
:are at the mercy of the Liberals; in the latter, at the
mercy of the Lords. Both the Liberals and the Lords
are—English, and the quality of their mercy is likely
“to be somewhat strained. :

Assuming that we do gain that position of qualified
command over the Liberals which I have just referred
to, what then? To demand sweeping constitutional
changes would be futile, for, with the best will in the
world, the Liberals could not fulfil such demands, so
long as the blue-blooded Chamber blocks the way. De-
volution, then? I think so. But let mo be nowise
misunderstood. I am not a Devolubionist; that is, I
«do not believe that Devolution will solve the Irish ques-
‘tion. But I do belicve that ten years more in the
+wilderness, with Devolution, would be far more toler-
able than ten years there without it. Some may think
“this a low view, but I do not; I make no pretence of
‘belonging to that heroic school of politicians, who be-
‘lieve that no bread is better than halfia.loaf. To take
‘Devolution is not to abandon Home Rule;: I should nof
take it, if it were. To refuse Devolution, in a. pet or
2 pout, would' be a folly which T hope: the Trish. Rarty
will not fall into. Afber all, Devolution. would simply

case the situation, pending the great se(;tlement.. We

ability and initiabive. If Devolution will give us that
hold, and that outlet, it will be something—except, per-
haps, in the oyes of those who prefer no bread to half -
a loaf 3

: One question more. What should. the Trish voter in
Great Britain do? It is a curious question. In theory
ghe Trish Parliamentary Party is altogether independent
of all English parties. Is it not odd, then, to be con-
fronted with the question, for which of them shéuld the
Trish olector in Great Britain vote? In the present case,
T think for neither. We do not want the Tories back—
just yet, anyhow. The Liberals are likely to have a
large enough majority without us; too large, 'Ifca):.
Why vote at all, then? T think the Irish voter in Bri-
tain had better lic low, unless circumstances, local or
otherwise, dictate some other course. ~There is certainly
not much use in voting for Liberal Imperialists, any-
how ; somuch is clear.

I fear my forecast as to what will happen should the
Liberals win their heart’s desire—an independent majo-
rity—will raise gloom in some minds, and perhaps vex
the souls of those who are filled with the old Irish un-
willingness' to believe the disagreable. Perhaps I am
too dark a prophet; but of this I feel fully assured—
that there will be no Mome Rule in the next Parlia-
nt. Bub that do ot necessarily mean that there
1 bo nothing. Aft

W all, the Liberals are steeped to
the lips in ameliorative promises, and upon these they
cannot utterly go back without incurring that taint of
odium which bad faith brings upon all parties, even
upon those with independent majorities  Again, a
large section of the Cabinet consists of men who have
always had a favourable leaning towards Irish claims,
ud the Liberal Unionists themselves have never pro-
fessed opposition to ameliorative measures other than
Home Rule. TLet us hope for the best. The General
Election will give us further light, and for that we must
wait. Iyaar.

further the cause of the language should make people
look round them in quest of some useful thing that has
not yet been done, and turn their attention and their
talents to that, rather than to be for ever falling over
cach other through their all trying to do the same
thing, no matter how often or how completely it has
been done before.

that neither pleases nor offends anybody, so very little
of the reality of Irish life is there in the whole thing.
Now ‘“Tom O’Kelly ”” is written with a glorious in-
difference to whether it pleases or offends anybody, and
by consequence it is more natural and convincing than
all the gentle tales that ever adorned the unoffending
pages of the Irish Monthly. ¢

Now for the bool itself. There is no more “ plot
in it than there is in real life itself—which only makes
it all the more natural and real. Indeed, T think it
should have been called “ The O’Kelly Family,”” rather
than ‘‘ Tom O’Kelly.”  That, however, is a detail.
The book, at all events, is a humorous document of Trish
life, rather than a novel with a plot of the * Monte
Cristo ”” or cven of the ““Tale of Two Cities””
type. Af 11, what plot is there in ‘“ Knocknagow’’ ?
Or, what is more to the purpose, what plot is there in
actual life? Nevertheless, the book begins and ends
with Tom O’Kelly, and as an illustration of the sur-
roundings and of the atmosphere (if an “ atmosphere ”
can be lustrated) in which many a yo Irish lad
cvolves his character, it will not easily be rivalled. As
ery man’s life is largely moulded when he is a lad,
and moulded mostly by the influence of the members
of his own family, and by that of his young com-
panions, it follows that a life-sketch of the youth him-
self will take in all this human environment, and in
“Tom O’Kelly” it does. We are accordingly shown
the father and mother of Tom, and we see them through
to their death. We are introduced, besides, to his
brothers and sisters, and their carcers are followed as
far as the turning point; thal , to such a point as
cnables us to guess pretty shrewdly what will be their
future. What more would you have? Nine-tenths of
the regulation novels do not give us so much, to say
nothing of the fact that their falsetto air, and their
jerrymandering of reality, reduces their intrinsic moral
value to a low point, while their lack of humour and
sincerity makes them, very often, unconscionably hard
te get through. The characters in the present story,
outside the O’Kelly family, are of course more briefly
sketched, but they throw a good deal of light on Irish
life, and many of them ring so real that they lock like
reality itself.  Tom’s father, Patrick O’Kelly, is of
sound Irish peasant stock, but is not himself a peasant,
having taken to the milling business. Late in life he
takes to politics, and then to drink; then to betting,
and thence to bankruptcy, and theuce, sobered but
heart-broken, to his grave. Mrs. O'Kelly is a saint,
soft-hearted and good-tempered, and with, perhaps, not
the least idea that she is a saint. She is an Irish type.
There are such women, and they are the real salt ‘of Ire-
land. They are Ireland’s credit balance as against the
public-house, and much other hidcousness and vulgarity
besides. = They are something that our ** superiors’
noever see, and. would not know how to value if they
did see. After all, Wilson Ygung and Company have
not drawn all of Catholic Treland.

After the father and mother, both in their own ways
sound and genuine, come the younger folk. Here we
aro on new ground, and in a different generation. Some
of the young folk are high-flyers at fashion, as they
understand i, and ambition an' accent and society.
Posed against all this we have the old man’s politics
and political friends-—aund cnomies; the old man’s
ignorance (of all outside the milling business) and his
peasant downrightness and honesty. The evolution.
of this situation and' of all its attendant characters, con-
stitutes the book, and it will be seen that such material
offers capital ground for a series of life-pictures,
humorous and convincing.

Tlumorous many of the pictures cortainly ave. For
oxample, the wedding-breakfast of  Miss Mary ' at
the Dublin hotel in an ecarly. chapter, and the account
of the Misses Williams (elderly young things;. but very
fashionable) concocting an “ At ¥lome,?” further on in
the hook.  Tu:the next chapter tlie account of Peter

O’Kelly looking () for employment is very diverting,
and }"et it veads very real. The bowing out of Miss
Alice’s suitor by her father is very droll, and the lan-
guage of t}w old man rings very true. There is very
much else in the book that might be mentioned for its
humorous quality, such as the doings of the youngsters,
and much of the political proceedings. Tom going up
to comfort Peter when the old man will not believe bhat
the latter has written a famous “ lettor to the papers '’
Is another very genuine bit. As for the ‘“move’’ of
the Extreme Party, and the characters of Stephen
Mooney and Peter Connell (ex-physical force men) 1
can certify to their truth to life, comic as they may
seem. T have known characters like them, especially
like Connell. I mention these bits, because to quote
any part of the book at length would make this article
too long. The chief strokes of satire in the story are
contained in some of the political parts, and in the
account of the Royal Hotel bar wd. ITere we see the
merally seamy side of Anglo-lvish town life, and wha

& joyless lot the hell-of-a-follow types of young men are
Thomas Malone, the whilom patriotic ballad-writer and

leader of gay youth, is well sketched, and in the end
of this chapter we get a fine bit of serious writing. The
pathetic part of the book is chicfly that recounting the
ceath of Mrs. O’Kelly, and the failure and death of
the old man himself. This much I can say—the
author’s pathos is as natural as his humour, and the
death of Mrs. O’Kelly is as easily recounted as the tale
of Peter, her son, ‘‘looking”’ for employment. Tf 1
might sum up the book in one sentence, I should say
that it shows us the farce that forms so large a part of
Irish life, the unobserved (and unrehearsed) comedy
that forms so much more of it, and the tragedy that
underlies most of it. It shows us the comedy of so
much that our people take seriously, and the serious-
ness of some things that we take very lightly indeed.
Were I to find any fault with the book, it might be,
perhaps, with some of the sharper satirical touches.
There is a seamy side to politics everywhere, and foolish
and undignified figures, too. After all, these bump-
tious, self-opinionated, and often muddle-headed pro-
vincials, do, collectively, effect something—Land Acts,
for instance—however funny they may seem taken
singly, and I had much rather take Uncle Michael to
my heart than the pasty-faced youths of cosmopolitan
civilisation to whom the oily Semite sells the lascivious
photograph on the sly. I had sooner have Patrick
O’Kelly any day, than the plutocrat distiller, who, with
a glorious galaxy of intellectual stars from the Unionist
Alliance, crossed over to England to represent to a Con-
servative caucus meeting the desirability of reducing
the Trish representation—he, the ecver-respectable,
Manufacturing Bung! However, this is merely saying
that the author is a bit over-severe, at times; it is not
saying that he is radically unjust. My only fear for
the book is that those who enjoy its drollery will not
always realise that some of the fun is poked at them-
sclves. Patrick O’Kelly and Uncle Michael may have
done queer things at that wedding-breakfast, but will
not many a reader—or readeress—of the book have
done equally funny things without cver realising it?
In a word, what I fear is that many, perhaps most,
readers of the book will see the fun, but not the humour
of it: They will laugh with the author, no doubt, but

A PLAY AND A SOLITUDE.

=\ O they do it all for their own amusement?—is the
question you ask yourself as you walk'fxwibyvfrom
the Abbey Theatre. “The White Cockade,” by Lady
Gregory, is one of the good things of the National
Theatre Society. Unlike some of the compositions they
produce—or rather used to produce—it is a play, not
a poem or a treatise. Its plot, its dialogue, and its in-
cidents are all excellent, and all the more wur@hy of
praise because an historical play is an extremely difficult
thing to write, and even Shakespeare was least success-
full in that province. Compared with such a drama as
the “Breed of the Treshams,” for example, which all
Dublin was flocking to see some weeks ago, *“ The White
Cockade ”’ was immeasurably superior, for it dealt in
realities, and not in stage conventions. Nor was the
acting deficient ; there was, perhaps, no one actor with
the technical skill of Harvey, and a few of the minor
characters suffered from amateurish tricks, but_the five
principals were quite up to a high professional stan-
dard. One thing only was lacking—an audience, and
that was lacking with a vengeance the night I was there.
The most striking .thing about the National Theatre
plays is the number of people who do not go to them.
Seemingly like characters of fable, its members have
been taken at their word. They have gone about say-
ing they do not want the public, that they do not wish
to make their plays a commercial success, but they desire
“fit audience tho’ few,”” and the like. They have also
closed their doors in the face of sixpenny patrons. Six
pennies will admit you to the Gaiety or the Royal, to
the Opera or to Esposito, but the uncommercial
theatre must have its shilling’s worth of flesh.  The
public has unhappily believed these pretensions, and
feeling that it were indelicate to force their company on
a household so exclusive, they have left the players to
perform in that Arctic solitude which they profess to
love. The result is decreased box-office receipts, and is—
or rather ought to be—greatly increased cost of heating.
Now it is really a pity to see such an excellent perform-
ance wasted, and by its unpopularity it defeats its own
cxpress object. For the best fruit of a high-class play
is that it should be talked about, thought about, and
looked forward to. In a properly regulated community
the theatre should be a focus of intellectuality, a living
criticism of life.  To a certain extent the commer-
cial " theatre fulfils that function, and a play acted by
Harvey or Robertson creates for some little time an
eddy of shallow reflections in the undeveloped minds of
our Britonised middle class. For at and after such an
entertainment they sip the philosophy of life with a tea-
spoon. The function of an Irish theatre should be a
noble one. But if the Abbey Theatre maintains its
aloofness and its unpopularity, it can never hope to be-
come a potent distributor of broader native ideas:
Lady Gregory’s play has an admirable unity. The:
coqu'nl idea 1s the contrast between the man, Sarsfield,
who is by nature the leadex of his people, and the pol-
troon, James, whom his lincage and their loyalty have
unfortunately placed in that position. The moment is
that fatal one when the Catholics of Ireland ruined
themselves in the Stuart cause, a blow from which they-
have never recovered. For Ireland is still paying in-
demnity for the campaign of the Boyne. The treat-
ment of James's character is similar to that adopted’
by Douglas Hyde in his little comedy, Rig Seamup,”
b.ut. is worked ‘out with greater delicacy and skill. Tra-
dition” ropresents that monarch as an abject coward,,

A beautiful and pathetic figure is the ¢ Poor
Lady ’—a distraught and ruined wanderer in the
Stuart cause, acted with admirable fecling and delicacy
by Mape Nic Suvtay  The loyal dovotion of this
poor female Lear, brings before us all that insane reli-
gion of Stuart loyalty which ruined our people in those
days. What a terrible thing was that devotion of the
many to the worthless one! No doubt, the treacherous
royal figure-head was at that time in Irish eyes tho
palladium of their religion, their freedom, and ecven
their nationality. But we may, in truth, be glad that
the drab-lined ideas of American democracy has in our
day displaced the deceptive glitter of the English con-
stitution. Tt is impossible to deseribe here with what
subtle art the poor worn figure and the wandering mind
of this withered devotee are made to express the fallacy
of those principles which, in their result, annihilated
our aristocracy, and gave over our commons to bondage
and long-continued persecution.

W. G. Fay and Sara Allgood as tippling Matthew
Kelleher and Mary, his practical and energetic wife,
gave us those admirable character sketches which we
always expect from them. Their parts on this occasion
seemed to suit them peculiarly well. The two Wil-
liamites represented histrionic talent promising, but
still untrained. The scenery was very excellent. In
truth, the one thing now needed is to procure specta-
tors, to give courage to the vulgar, and withdraw that
artistic interdict which at present scares them away.
Once make them believe these are real plays, and real
actors performing for the benefit of a real audience,
